: GURRENT AFFAIRS,

The Directors of the ill-fated National Assurance Co.

"are all men of repute and standing. Yet, where is the

National Assurance Co. now? After all, names, even

' though they be largely those of Unionists and non-Catho-

lics do not necessarily, even in Ireland, command success.
The National Assurance Co. debacle is a rude and cruel
awakening to many unfortunate ruined and partly
ruined shareholders. Mr. Joseph Todhunter Pim occu-
pied the chair at the last meeting, and amongst the
other ‘directors present was Frederick C. Pilkington,
Esq., J.P., D.L., also director of the Great Southern
and Western Railway. Now, if in spite of the great

 array of highly respectable names that figure in the list

of Directors of this unfortunate National Assurance Co.

the shareholders had had grit enough to take action long
ago, what might the result be to-day? If any share-
holder of the National Assurance Company had sug-
gested four or five years ago that a shareholders’ com-
mittee of investigation should have been appointed to
look into the concern, what an outery there would have
been! What! Did he want to throw a doubt on the
financial ability of Frederick C. Pilkington, D.L., J.P.,
director of the G. S. & W. Railway, over which a real
baronet now presides, of Jonathan Hogg, D.L., a
director of the Bank of Ireland, of Arthur E. Kinahan,
director of a whiskey concern, of Joseph Todhunter
Pim, a director of the Bank of Ireland, of William
Robertson, director of the Bank of Ireland! Why, the
‘“tames”’ and the Ascendancy would throw up their
hands in horror, and the Irish Times might write a
philosophic article. If anyone said that there was a
possibility that, were the eminent directors allowed to
run the Company at their own sweet will, that a large
part of the concern would be landed at last in the lap
of Yorkshire yokels he would be denounced as vulgar,
scurrilous and unmannerly. In the course of the re-
marks of the chairman at the last meeting (Mxr. Joseph
Todhunter Pim), that eminent financier said:—‘‘In
rising to propose that the report and statement of ac-
counts be adopted I need scarcely say that it gives the
directors unqualified pain and regret in being obliged
to put before the shareholders such a statement as to
the condition of the company’s affairs.”” Who would
have thought of such a thing—in view of the eminent
respectability of the directors—four years ago. It is
enough to permanently shake the faith of the common,
low-down investor in eminently respectable names. The
Great Southern and Western Railway is Chairmanned by
a real baronet, and the names of the directors are most
respectable. Here is an eminently respectable sentence
from the speech of Joseph Todhunter Pim:—‘‘ By our
United States Treaty we have lost £106,000 from 1898
to 1904 inclusive, of which sum £84,000 has accrued
since June, 1899. By our own direct. United States
business we have lost £64,000 including £28,000 at Bal-
timore.””  That is nice reading for low-down share-
holders who have been accustomed to look up humbly
and reverently to eminently respectable directors’
names.

A sharcholder, Mr. W. H. Jounes (Manchester) in the
course of his remarks as reported, said:—“It was no
exaggeration to say that the report for 1904 was the
most melancholy statement of the results of mismanage-
ment that has ever been published in the annals of fire
insurance. When the late manager died, the company
was none too strong, but its capital of £100,000 was in-
tact and its reserves amounted to £108,454, being
nearly 40 per cent. of the income. There was then no
reason whatever to think that the company would not,
with prudent management, become stronger year by
year, indeed this view was most sanguinely expressed in
your reports to the shareholders for the years 1898,
1900 and 1901. What is the position at the end of
these seven or eight years? The Reserve Fund of
£108,4564 has disappeared after accumulating for
seventy-six years. Not satisfied with that, the direc-
tors offered a tempting bait for more money by induc-
ing shareholders to pay calls in advance, and to favour
them with 5 per cent. interest never earned, and there-
fore, no right to have been paid. These calls increased
the capital to £145,185. Notwithstanding all this, we
have a debit balance to face of £172,304. During
these eight years of so-called management the loss has
averaged £35,000 a year.”’

We have :eceivecl a copy of the Prospectus of the 
Connacht Insh School, which has been established at 
-Mount Pa;rtry, Co. Mayo, for the training of Teachers 
and teachmg of Irish. 
The President is His Grace the 
Archbishop of Tuam, the secretary is Mr. Joseph A. 
G~ynn, Beech :ijouse, Tuam, and there is a strong and 
representative committee. 
As Principal Teacher the 
Connacht School has happily secured the services of 
Michael Breathnach, late of the London Gaelic League. 
• There will be two courses, the first beginning 15th June 
and ending 31st July; the second beginning 1st August 
and ending llt).i September. 
Funds, of course, are 
necessary for the adequate carrying out of the scheme 
of the· Committee, and we- hope that the appeal for 
financial help made by the Committee will meet with 
a hearty and generous response. 
Certainly t}1e Irish Parliamentary Party have every 
reason to feel honoured and pleased at the reception 
accor~ed to their chairman at the Vatican, by his Holi-
ness tlie Pope. According to a report in the Freeman : -
" His Holiness spoke in the kindest ter.ms of the work 
of the Irish Parliamentary Party. He expressed his 
complete sympath)Z with the objects and the labours of 
the Party. 
' I recognise the Irish Parliamentary 
Party,' he said, ' as the defender of the C'atholic reli-
gion, because that is the National religion, and it is the 
• National Party.' The struggles of the Party by lawful 
and peaceful means to win political liberty for Ireland, 
and to obtain the full civic rights for the Irish people, 
denied to them at present, had, continued his Holiness, 
his deep sympathy, and his blessing." 
The portrait of 
the Pope which His Holiness presented to Mr. John 
• Redmond, bore the following inscription : -_" To my 
Beloved Son, John RMmond, Leader of the Insh Party 
in the House of Commons, with a wish that he, together 
with his equally beloved colleagues, using all legal and 
peaceful means, may win that liberty which makes for 
the welfare of the Catholic Church and of the whole 
country, We impart Our Apostolic Benediction, wit h 
particular affection. • 
"From the Vatican, 27th April, 1905. 
"PIUS P.P." 
ln the course of Reuter's account of the interview we 
read:-" The audie11;ce lasted for a considerable time. 
The Pope inquired with keen interest into the religiou~, 
political, and· industrial development of Ireland. His 
Holiness said the Irish National Party was the defender 
of the Catholic religion in Ireland, because that was the 
National religion in Ireland, and it was a National 
Party. Its efforts to win liberty for Ireland and full 
civil and religious rights for the Irish people by la,wful 
and peaceful means had his sympathy and blessing, and· 
he sent his Apostolic Benediction to the msmbers of 
the Irish Party and their families." 
• 
The Return which, owing to Mr. McVeagh's action, 
has been ordered by Parliament, is, or rather wil~ be, 
a document of incalculable value in the fight for Toler-
ance Justice and Fair Play in the country. 
Though 
. di:ffi~ulties m;y be placed in the way of a full_ and co_m-
plete return in conse9-uence of the broad hn1:t which 
the Govel·nment has given to those from whom mfonµa-
tion may be asked, yet the return will prove invaluable, 
and the . information that is lacking from the Govern-
ment Return can, we have no doubt, be easily made up 
by private enterprise. 
But the fight for Tolerance, 
Justice and Fair Play must be largely ':ought out in 
detail in Ireland, and a special or-ganisation of Catho-
lici;-the non-Catholics have a network of organisations 
.:....for the purpose of asserting the civil rights and de-
fending the temporal and economic interests of Catho-
lics, is badly needed-indeed, it is an obvious necessity. 
We have received the following interesting communi-
cation:-" Dear Mr. Editor,-We, the Idolators of the 
D.M.P., as you so aptly have styled us, are pleased to 
see you again coming forward to champion the cause of 
Tolerance and Fair Play . . Since the appearance of your 
first article, many changes, which are b·eneficial to t~e 
Catholic members of the force, have been effected, and 
we have very little doubt that if you take the matter 
seriously in hand, you w-ill effect as great a change as 
you have done in other pu?lic ~odies, ~otably the 
Railways, of Ireland; as the bigots•m a .po~ice force are 
no more able to stand the light of publicity than any 
other public body. 
A change of constab_le~ . emplo}'.ed 
on special duty has taken place in the Div1S1on which 
was the subject-of your first article, and there has been 
a noticeable falling off in the number of " Saved " con-
stables obtaining soft jobs ' to suit their delicate state of 
health.' Another change which benefits the ' Idola-
trous ' constables is that the extra work entailed on 
night duty has been done away with; but quite recently 
an appointment was made of a constable to the stafi of 
the Police Court; and although an " Idolator '' was 
recommended by his Superintendent, the appointmen,t 
was filled from the ranks of " the Saved,'' contrary· to 
Sir John's edict, viz., seniority." 
The following also is taken from this strange message 
that has reached us :- " Of the many cases of impudent 
bigotry in the force there is no parallel to that oJ the 
apointment of the Assi'stant Drill Instructor in the 
Depot, Kevin Street . This appointment was formerly 
filled by an ' Idolator '-Constable Langtry-who was 
in every respect qualified, holding a first-class certificate 
frcm military schools for D1·ill Instruction and Gym-
nastics, also a second-class educational certificate in the 
force. 
This man was removed to make room for one of 
the " Saved "-a man holding neither educational ,nor 
drill certificates, and who had been for some years pre-
viously a member of the band. ,The result of this was 
that the ' Idolator ' constable, seeing the futility of 
gaining advancement in a service where such barefaced • 
bigotry prevailed, decided ·on leaving the force and has 
gone to South America, notwithstanding the fact that 
this man's brotuer is Superintendent of the G. Division. 
vVhat can other ' Iclolators ' expect who have no in-
fluence "

We have received the following letter with reference 
to " ·the King" incident at the Teacher's Dinner 
at 
Sligo:-" Dear 
Sir,-! 
wish 
to 
draw 
your 
attention to the first toast drunk at the Teachers' 
Congress Banquet in Sligo, last night, ·namely, ·"The 
King." 
The local .committee invited a number of 
guests, not one of whom knew that this toast was to be 
drunk, and when they came into the banquet hall, they 
decided not to remain unless they got a guarantee that 
the toast would not be drunk, and made the decision 
known to the committee. 
The committee refused to 
give the guarantee, and about half ·the guests left 
immediately. 
Others of the guests left before the 
toast, others sat down whilst it was being drunk, and 
others joined' in the toast. Perhaps you would com-
ment on t4,e occureuce in the LEADER. 
Surely the

164

teachers should not invite nationalists to drink the
party toast of England’s Faithful Garrison ; and surely,
too, the teachers themselves as a body do not belong to
the garrison, and surely they should therefore take
more care that this toast should not be drunk at any
of their future re-unions.—Yours sincerely,

“A Guesr.”

It is a serious state of things that the Committee of
the Irish National Teachers’ Congress should insist
upon toasting “ The King’ at their banquet. Even
yet in Ireland “ God Save the King’’ means for the
most part, “ Damn the ¢ Idolators’ and Nationalists,””
and the Teachers’ Association as a professedly “ non-
political *’ body, had no business in dragging that highly
inflamable toast into their festive proceedings; they
should have left that toast to the Dublin members of
the British Institute of Journalists. In the Freeman’s
Journal of April 28th, we read :—“ A striking incident
took place before the dinner commenced. A number of
the invited guests, including some clergymen, having
observed that the first toast on the printed programme
was that of ‘ The King,” decided to leave immediately,
and they did so, being unwilling to participate in a
function of which the toast was a feature. Those who
left at this period did not return. Others disassociated
themselves from the toast by leaving the dining hall
before it was proposed and remaining outside until it
had been disposed of. When the toast was actually
given by the President, the majority of those present,
guests and Congress delegates, remained seated, choosing
this third method of indicating their non-participation
in this particular item of the programme. There were
repeated cries of ‘ Seats.” The President passed hastily
to the next toast on the list, which, with the succeeding
ones, was enthusiastically honoured.”” We hope the
Freeman’s Journal will give its readers an account of
how “ The King ’’ is received at the next dinner of the
Dublin and other members of the British Institutes of
Journalists! There was certainly some spirit and back-
bone displayed at the National School Teachers’ Con-
gress Dinner, at Sligo, for several of the invited guests
declined to take part in the proceedings, others made
themselves scarce until “The King’’ was disposed of,
and others protested by remaining seated whilst the
toast was being drunk by the true blues’’ and, no
doubt, the “tames.”

The sixth toast at this partly “loyal ’’ gathering was
that of the Gaelic League. It lagged rather far behind
the toast of “ The King.”” Dr. Hyde responded in an
eloquent speech which we are glad to see is rather fully
reported in the Freeman’s Journal. In the course of
his reply to the toast, Dr. Hyde said:— The motto of
the Gaelic League was the open door to all Irishmen,
no matter what their religion, no matter what their
politics might be (loud applause).” We may be ex-
cused for saying that that comes upon us by surprise
just now, for we have a recollection that a member of
the Dublin Corporation and a member also of a branch
of the Gaelic League, a man by the name of Dr.
McWalter, was recently treated to anything but the
motto of the Gaelic League as described by Dr. Douglas
Hyde! and ‘instead of the “open door’’ a gang of
criminal street ruffians, whose conduct the majority of
the Executive at last meeting have ostentatiously re-
fused to denounce, “ held him up ’’ at the Dublin Lan-
guage Procession. It is true that he had two guests in
his carriage, one an eminent solicitor and well-known
public man, Mr. John Gore, and the other “a person
obnoxious to Irish Ireland’’ by the name of D. P.
Moran, editor of this journal. - These three were
treated to foot-pad tactics by the blackguards acting
professedly in the name of the Gaelic League, and the
Executive Committee of that Organisation has, in a

 sense, attempted to affiliate the League with this street
blackguardism by refusing to denounce and apologise
for the criminal outrage openly committed in the name
of the Gaelic League. Yet at this meeting in Sligo we
"are told by the President of the Gaelic League that
““The motto of the Gaelic League was the open door to

THE LEADER.

May 6, 1905.

all Irishmen, no matter what their religion,
no matter what their politics might be.”
Which is right, the President or the majority of the
Executive? The President says the open door;’’ ‘the
majority at the last meeting of the Executive side with
criminal street blackguardism and the “shut door.””
Dr. Hyde was not at the last meeting of the Executive
and as he stands for the ‘“ open door,”” he is presum-
ably at variance with the vote of the Executive. What
does Dr. Douglas Hyde, as President of the Gaelic
League think?

Dr. Douglas Hyde, the President of the Gaelic
Lecague went on: —* Upon their platform, Catholic and
Protestant and Dissenter, stood together, and they were
knit in bonds of the closest friendship. That word was
too weak—they were knit together in bonds of a
brotherly love that had been unexampled in Ireland
in recent times.”” For our part we are inclined to
think that Dr. McWalter, T.C., would in one sense, em-
phatically agree that Irishmen on a recent Sunday
“ were knit together in bonds of a brotherly love that
had been unezampled in Ireland in recent times.” Cer-
tainly it was an “ unexampled *’ kind of brotherly love!
If the foot-pad tactics of which Dr. McWalter was one
of the victims, and which have not been denounced by
the Executive, are examples of “ brotherly love,”” well
then let us turn off the supply; the country has had

THE LEADER.

“May 6, 1905,

BYE-WAYS IN IRISH INDUSTRY.
A CARLOW BOOT FACTORY.

T is now four years and a half ago since we wrote
owr sixth article of our “Nation Building”

series. That article which appeared in our issue of
November 10th, 1900, was on Boots. We find, on
referring back to it, that our approximate calculation,
assuming that all the leather used in the boots had to
be imported—and as a matter of fact that was not, or is
not, so—was that about £800,000 a year would go to
shoeing the population of Ireland. Our information
at the time was that not one-tenth of that sum was
kept in the country, or in other words, that £720,000
was lost to it because Ireland did not shoe itself. - Since
then a considerable improvement has been effected.
Winstanley’s is the largest boot factory in Ireland, and
in our Christmas Number, December, 1903, they an-
nounced that they employed over 300 workers in their
factory, and found that number insufficient to execute
all orders, and added, “skilled labour urgently ve-
quired.”” The Lee Boots, of Cork; Hearn and Cahill,
of Waterford; Williamson’s, of Belfast, no doubt also
considerably expanded their output; but in view of our
approximate calculation four and a half years ago, that
even assuming that Ireland imported all the leather
used in the boots made—which she does not, and the
tanning industry is capable of great development—that
the country paid to foreign makers £720,000 annually
on the head of boots, there is a great margin left to be
worked upon.  There is a good deal of picking on
£720,000 a year, and notwithstanding the considerable
advance that has been made in the way of the Irish
consumers giving up playing the fool by supporting
foreign boots when Irish boots were to be had as good
and as cheap, there is still a great deal of work left for
the common-sense Irish boot-buyer to do. Every reader
of the LEADER is a purchaser of boots, and we put it to
the intellect of each one of them that they should de-
mand in the shops in which they deal that they be
enabled to sample Irish-made boots that are fighting
in competition with foreign boots. If they find them
right as regards quality and price, they should most un-
doubtedly give them the preference. If our readers
find that the shops in which they deal do not offer a
fair opportunity to their customers to sample and use
their own judgment on the merits of Irish-made boots
as against British and foreign boots, they ought, in fair-
ness to the industrial interests of the country, to
speak firmly to, and what is more, act firmly with,
such shop-keepers. Irish boots are entitled to a fair
field and no favour in the shops of Ireland that deal in
boots, and our readers should see as far as they reason-
ably may do so, that they get it. The boot manufac-
turers of Ireland have no right to ask the public for as
much as a brass farthing for nothing, but they are on-
titled to a preference from the Irish buying public on
their merits. We put the case this way :—It is common
sense that the whole country benefits by the prosperity
of the boot-making industry; the common-sense public
who read the LEADER are not asked to make any sacri-
fice for the sake of the special boot-making interest, but
they are, as common-sense, thinking people, asked to
see that they do not make a sacrifice for the benefit of
British and American boot manufacturers; they are
asked to see that Irish-made boots get a fair field. We
repudiate the idea that they are entitled to any favour
beyond that, other things being equal, Irish boots, as
a matter of common sense, should ‘get the preference
from Irish boot wearers. We say that every Irish boot
shop should be in a position to show its customers fair
samples of the products of the several Irish boot
factories. :

“incontestably proved that part-singing was known to the
Irish.in the seventh century.” Again, Mr. Flood quotes
from O'Curry (p. viii.) :—“ Walker seems to have been the
sport- of every pretender to antiquarian knowledge, but
more especially the dupe of an unscrupulous person of the
name of Beauford.” And on p. 58 we get some information
about the neums of the Irish. Now, what is the source of
this information, if not “an unscrupulous person of the
name of Beauford ” ?

Under this second aspect, too, the second half of the book
is much better. Indeed, it is a veritable storehouse of well-
authenticated information. I have some misgivings, how-
ever, about the aftempts to date some of the Irish melodies.
Gn p. 121, for instance, the author assigns Seaghan 0'Duibhir
an Gleanna to the last quarter of the sixteenth century. But
the reasons he gives go only to show that the poem belongs to
that date. They do not prove that the tune is not very much
older. It is similar with a number of other melodies. There
is some difficulty with regard to the air Tighearna Maigheo,
p- 205. Here we have the direct testimony of Charles
Q’Conor that the tune was composed by the harper, Thady
Keenan. This is evidence that the historian cannot easily
set aside. Still internal evidence is very much against it.
The tune is strictly pentatonic—the b natural occurring twice
in the usual setting is evidently interpolated. It seems to
me absolutely impossible that such an air could have been
composed at the end of the seventeenth century. I am
strongly inclined to date it back to pre-Christian times. The
difficuity, then, is to explain away O’Conor’s testimony. I
think, however, we might easily suppose a misunderstanding.
Thady Keenan may have said, ““ That is my air,” meaning
that lLe alone had the tradition of it, and that David O’Mur-
ray got it from him, while Charles O’Conor took it as meaning
that- Keenan had composed it. Anyhow the question de-
serves discussion.

In connection with Seaghan O’Duibhir an Gleanna, Mr.
Flood remarks :—‘“ The very construction of the air seems
to point to the second half of the sixteenth century, rather
than the middle of the seventeenth century.” It is very
much to be regretted that he gives us no further light on this
question. After all the Irish melodies are the main item
in the subject of Irish music, and a history of Irish music
ought, therefore, in the main to be a history of the Irish
airs. But ahout that we have too little in Mr. Flood’s book.
Perhaps the time has not yet come for such a history. But
any little light on the subject would be most welcome.

T must end here. I have thought it my duty to utter this
criticism on the book. But I repeat once more that the au-
thor deserves great credit for having given us such valuable
information’ as the book contains. Every Irishman who can

~ afford to spend a few shillings on Art ought to buy a copy.

The publishers have done their part excellently. The only
objection I could raise here would be that the leaves are
badly cut. I hope sincerely that the sale of the book will en-
courage similar enterprise.

H. BEWERUNGE.

Five years ago, under the pressure which the public

sand a year, it may be surmised what a big experiment
was at issue. Yet, as we have just said, the Ag_mcul-
tural Department made no serious attempt to arrive at
a conclusion whether it was worth while for the farmer
to attempt the growth of the plant, or the manufacturer
to make it into a commercial article fit to smoke. The
Department dallied and dawdled, and attempted the
experiment in anything but a con amore spirit. 5

Of course, based as the present system of taxation is,
on a duty almost six times the value of the tobacco leaf
in the cured but unmanufactured state, the Revenue
Departments concerned with the collection of the duty
naturally should have a determining voice in the regu-
lations dealing with the growth of the plant until it
became cured leaf fit for the manufacturer.  Practi-
cally, however, the Inland Revenue authorities for the
past five years, with a wonderful generosity, handed over
to the Irish Agricultural Board the monopoly of grow-
ing the plant under very easy conditions indeed. No
other persons were allowed to grow the crop until the
sanction of Sir Horace Plunkett’s Board was obtained.
They selected the persons and the places. It had to be
grown under their direction and the supervision of their
expert, and if any credit was to be gained, or.any
failure made, the Department, and the Department
alone, was to be credited with the success or the failure
of the experiment, as they alone were given the
monopoly. i

The Agricultural Department had everything in its
favour. In selecting its officers to conduct the experi-
ments it had the usual carte blanche which is given to
all Government Departments under such circumstances.
Through the international courtesy which obtains be-
tween most of the European and American nations, the
Department had the option of selecting the best ex-
perts which money and a wealthy Government could
purchase. As far as is known, too, the Department
was not restricted in funds. It had the choice of the
four provinces in Ireland to select where the crop would
be grown. Further, it had the advantage—if its ex-
perts had taken the trouble—to look up the matter, of
knowing the six or seven counties in Ireland where
tobacco growing flourished and paid large profits some
seventy years ago. Yet with all these aids and advan-
tages, until last year, the tobacco growing experiment
was a miserable failure, and certainly reflected no credit:
on the Department uuder whose wing the experiments
were carried on.

I have just read the little book " How Mary Made 
Home Happy," by Kathleen Ferguson, and consider it 
a very useful book, and one that ought to- be in the 
hands of every girl over ten years of age. It describes 
how two little girls, aged twelve and ten years respec-
tively, had their first lessons in domestic economy in 
school; how, on their return home, they were able 'to do 
the things they had been taught. 
The book tells how 
to go about everything-to make the soda bread, to be 
clean and tidy, to cook a nice little plain dinner, to 
clean all kitchen utensils, to ·turn out a bedroom, and . 
leave it perfectly clean. 
Laundry work is not for-
gotten. The way to go about a day's washing is d~-
ecribed admirably, the sorting of the clothes, the steep-
ing, washing, and ironing of same. 
The papering of 
the house is also treated in this compact little book-
and gardening· on a · small scale. 
The book is very inte-
resting, and from the girl of ten to the mistress of the 
house ought to be re.ad. It should be in schools, and 
distributed amongst the children ; it will make the little 
ones enthusiastic and anxious to learn.

HE “Vision of MacConglinne,”” of which the
book I am reviewing is a version in modern
Irish, by Father Peter O’Leary, is a unique wonder-
tale in Middle Irish, of which two versions are extant.
A long and elaborate version is given in the Leabhar
Breac, a manuscript of the fourteenth century, and a
shorter version occurs in a MS. preserved in T.C.D.
Library, (H, 3, 18). Both these versions have been
edited and translated by Dr. K. Meyer, and are now
some fifteen years before the public. Father O’Leary’s
g version follows mainly the longer or Leabhar Breac nar-
- rative, though he introduces some particulars from the
shorter account. His version is not a clese moderni-
zation of the old tale, departing as it does from it in
many particulars; nevertheless, it hangs together quite
consistently, and is extremely readable. The style is
masculine and unconventional and the interest well
sustained ; the actual “Vision’ being perhaps the
least interesting part of the book. The story relates
how Cathal MacFinguine, king of Munster, who was
possessed by the Demon of Gluttony, was cured by Mac-
2 Conglinne, a distinguished scholar from Leath Chuinn.
_The Demon of Gluttony got entrance into Cathal in this
_ way:—Cathal was in love with Ligach, daughter of
Maeldun, king of Aileach in the North, and she re-
turned his affection, though neither had seen the other.
To prove the sincerity of her attachment, she used to
. send presents of apples, kernels and sweetmeats to
Cathal. When her brother Fergal was informed of
this he sent for her and asked her to declare the truth.
Fearing his curse she did so. He then directed her to
send the next present through himself to Cathal. This
she did. But Feargal, through the instrumentality of
a “Pean Pireos,” put charms and spells on the viands
and despatched them to Cathal, giving orders to his
messengers to entreat the Munster king in the most
‘solemn manner to eat them at once. Cathal did accord-
ingly, and from these charmed viands arose the Demon

treatment in the guest-house of the monks, and extem-

umbrage. IHe is sentenced to be executed, but escapes
his doom in the hope that a vision which he has related
before the assembled nobles of Cork would, if recited
before Cathal, cure him of his devouring greed. =~ Mac-
Conglinne sets out for the house of Pichan, where the
king is to be entertained at night. At dinner, by an
artifice, he attracts the notice of the king, on whom he
prevails to fast that night in order to free him from the
malediction pronounced against him by the monks of
Cork. It was a great favour from one torn internally
by the Demon of Gluttony. But MacConglinne wanted
more. He prevailed on Cathal to prolong his fast, and
when the lengthened fast had weakened him beyond the
power of resistance he had him securely bound. As
Cathal lay thus unable to move hand or foot, MacCon-
glinne began to recite his Vision in his ear, pausing every
now and then to present a morsel of the most savoury
meat to the king’s lips, and withdrawing it again after
a tantalising fashion. The continuous repetition of this.
process was more than the Demon of Gluttony within
the king could stand. Accordingly, as the recitation of
the Vision draws to a close. when MacConglinne pre-
sents a delicious morsel to the king’s lips, the Demon,
fearing a recurrence of his disappointment, jumps forth
from the king’s mouth in the shape of a newt, fas- S

Thus,” says the shorter version, ‘was Cathal
MacFl_ngnll,ne cured from his craving and MacConglinne
hcnoured: This story, of which I have only given a
bz}re outline, and which is delightfully told in Father
O’Leary’s book, may well induce us to reflect on the
ravages being made by the Demon of Drink amongst us
at the present day. A considerable number of the
- adolescent and adult population not only of Munster,
but of all Ireland are to-day cursed by an unearthly
craving for alcoholic spirits. = This craving is so insati-
able, so destructive to the spiritual and temporal wel-
fa_re,. not only of the individual, but of those
within the sphere of his influence, that it may be justly
termed the “ Drink Demon.”” This Demon ravages at
one and the same time the towns and cities, the vil-
lages and cottages of the land. The millions of pounds
to which the national drink bill annually amounts is
only a small fraction of the sum total of the tribute he
exacts. Who can reduce to money the loss sustained
when so many thousands of human souls are turned
away from the paths of innocence and virtue and
sunk into the hopeless depths of vice; when so many
adults have their constitution undermined and transmit
incurable diseases to future generations; when thou-
sands of the young and gifted of our race have their pros-
pects in life blighted ; when such vast areas of our cities
and large towns become “ power-houses’’ of moral and
physical disease ; when the.homes of so many toilers be-
come little better than hells on earth? Who can esti-
mate the loss in ruined character, in disappointed hope,
in neglected duty, in blighted ambition to the in-
dividual and.the race? Who can estimate all this and
reduce it to pounds sterling of the realm? Woe to
that realm whose security for such a large part of its
revenue consists in the permanent hold the Drink
Demon has on the minds and hodies of the people.
The ravages of this Demon are so vast and are increas-
ing at such a rate that, did I not believe we have suffi-
cient national grit left in us to apply a remedy, T
should forthwith despair of my country. But I de-
cline to believe that the evil is incurable. In the story
the Demon admits that it was through the intercession
of ‘“that little fair woman from the Curragh’ by
which is meant St. Bridget, that he loosed his hold
of Cathal. So, too, at the present day. The power of
prayer, influence of religion and of moral restraint must
form part of the cure.

keep the high and saving ideal of self-restraint before
the minds of the people. In the story, Cathal did not
fast alone, MacConglinne and the entire household
fasted with him. So, too, the exorcism of the nation
from the drink demon can never be effected until a
large and influential portion of the population consent
without any personal necessity to observe the fast of
total abstinence in conjunction with, and as an encour-
agement to their afflicted fellow-countrymen. The more
influential and exalted, the more persevering and self-
sacrificing these voluntary fasters from drink, the more
speedy and effective will be the national cure. The
clergy and religious communities should be in the van
of this great national expiatory movement, but the vir-
tuous, high-minded and enlightened laity who love their
country should not be far behind. The humblest toiler
in the land has his own place in this great army of a
nation’s regenerators. To me it is as clear as noqn-day
that a national exorcism can never be effected without
an earnest, widespread, persevering movement of total
abstinence. But more than this is necessary. In the
story, Cathal is bound hand and foot and kept from
food in the final stages of the cure. So, too, the Drink
Demon can never be expelled until the drunkard is
restrained by prudent, salutary, rigidly enforced legis-
lation. The evil has gone too far to be remedied with-
out strong legislative action. To come to particulars,
